description: BoomBrand brand intelligence framework — derived from analyzing 10 brands across 6 archetypes. This is the knowledge base that powers voice discovery, scoring, and auditing.
core_finding: POV (point of view) is the single strongest predictor of voice quality. Every brand scoring A or A+ has a sharp, controversial belief about their category. Every brand scoring C or below has a diluted or absent POV. Without POV, voice is just style — easily copied, never owned.

voice_interchange_format.description: Standard schema for passing brand voice between AI systems. Any tool consuming this format can write in the brand voice without additional context.

must_include: one good vs bad writing example
must_not_include: generic instructions any brand could use
style advice that sounds like copywriting 101
anything a competitor could use unchanged
system_prompt_spec.validation: If it could appear in any brand's style guide unchanged — rewrite it

scoring_rubric.description: Four dimensions, 25 points each, total 100. Apply to any brand voice.

pov_strength.description: How sharp, specific, and controversial is the brand's point of view?

scores.25: Controversial, specific belief that would make some people disagree. Nobody else in the category says this.
scores.15: Clear perspective but safe. True, but not surprising.
scores.5: Mission statement dressed as POV. Aspirational but toothless.
scores.0: No discernible POV. Could be anyone.

pov_strength.test: Would a competitor be uncomfortable if you said this about their category? If no — score lower.

vocabulary_ownership.description: How many words and phrases does this brand genuinely own?

scores.25: 3+ words/phrases uniquely associated with this brand. Customers use them without prompting.
scores.15: Some distinctive vocabulary but significant category bleed.
scores.5: Mostly category vocabulary. Few owned terms.

vocabulary_ownership.test: Read 10 sentences with brand name removed. Can you tell who wrote it? If no — score lower.

signature_moves.description: Are there specific, teachable rhetorical patterns unique to this brand?

scores.25: 2+ named, teachable patterns. A new writer can learn and replicate them.
scores.15: Consistent style but patterns not distinct enough to name.

signature_moves.test: Can you name a rhetorical move and give an example of it? If no — score lower.

examples.Basecamp: Naming the thing everyone thinks but won't say

consistency.description: Does the voice hold across channels, registers, and time?

sub_dimensions.cross_channel: Pricing page = blog = job listings = support docs = social
sub_dimensions.cross_register: Shifting from punchy to authoritative still sounds like same brand
sub_dimensions.temporal: Voice held over 2+ years without committee dilution
sub_dimensions.actions_match_voice: Business decisions back up what the voice claims

scores.25: Same voice everywhere, always. Actions match claims. Has held for years.
scores.15: Consistent in marketing. Breaks down in product/support/hiring content.
scores.5: Voice exists in some content. Absent in others. Inconsistent over time.
scores.0: Different voice in every context. No through-line.

consistency.test: Compare homepage copy to a job listing to a support doc. Same brand? If no — score lower.

A_plus.pattern: Sharp POV + total commitment + specific vocabulary ownership + actions match voice
characteristics: Signature moves teachable to any writer
Business model backs up the voice claims

A.pattern: Clear POV + strong voice + minimal category vocabulary leakage
characteristics: POV clear enough to polarize slightly
Some category vocabulary but mostly owned

B.pattern: POV exists but safe + good style + some category vocabulary
characteristics: Perspective clear but won't make anyone uncomfortable
Voice consistent in marketing, variable elsewhere
Clear style but patterns not fully teachable

C.pattern: POV diluted + inconsistent channels + significant category vocabulary
characteristics: POV exists in founding content, averaged out in current
Voice breaks down in product and support content

D_F.pattern: No POV + committee writing + all category vocabulary + banned openers
characteristics: Could be any brand in the category
Features as voice rather than POV as voice

category_challenger.description: Exists to fight a category convention. The enemy IS the category.
category_challenger.voice_trap: Becoming the thing you're fighting as you scale
category_challenger.pov_pattern: The [category] has been [doing wrong thing] for [time]. We exist to prove that's not necessary.

opinionated_tool.description: Has strong beliefs about how work should be done. Constraint is the product.
opinionated_tool.voice_trap: Arrogance without the product quality to back it up
opinionated_tool.pov_pattern: Most [tools] are designed for [wrong person]. We designed for [right person] and that changes everything.

character_driven.description: The brand IS a character. Voice and character are inseparable.
character_driven.voice_trap: Character getting stale or inconsistently applied across channels
character_driven.pov_pattern: [Category] doesn't have to be [boring thing]. We're proof.

mission_led.description: The cause IS the product. Business model backs up the belief.
mission_led.voice_trap: Preachiness — saying you care more than showing you care
mission_led.pov_pattern: [Industry] has optimized for [wrong thing]. We've built our entire business around [right thing] instead.

community_brand.description: User voice IS brand voice. Community language colonizes the brand.
community_brand.voice_trap: Brand coherence problem — no single person can articulate what the brand sounds like
community_brand.pov_pattern: We're not going to tell you how to use this. Here's what it can do.

committee_brand.description: Voice averaged across too many writers. POV diluted by scale.
committee_brand.voice_trap: Mistaking volume for voice — more content makes the problem worse
committee_brand.fix: Identify the 3-5 pieces of content that still have POV. Extract the editorial rules. Apply them as the new standard.

established_brand.trigger: Has URL + has brand footprint (reviews, press, social presence)
established_brand.research_focus: What does the internet say vs what they say about themselves
established_brand.conversation_arc: Excavate and codify what already exists
established_brand.output_type: Descriptive — here is your voice, made official
established_brand.key_question: What do your best customers say about you that your website doesn't?

new_brand.trigger: Has URL + little or no footprint (fewer than 3 Reddit mentions, no G2 profile)
new_brand.research_focus: Category white space + founder DNA + competitor voice audit
new_brand.conversation_arc: Origin mining + aspiration + enemy identification + permission slip
new_brand.output_type: Prescriptive — here is the voice you should grow into
new_brand.key_question: If you knew no investors were watching — how would you actually describe what you're building?

pivot_brand.trigger: Established brand explicitly wanting to change voice
pivot_brand.research_focus: Old voice audit + what equity to preserve + gap to goal state
pivot_brand.conversation_arc: What happened → where are you going → what stays → transition map
pivot_brand.output_type: Transitional — from/to voice map with transition rules
pivot_brand.key_question: What's the one thing a loyal customer would notice if it disappeared — and feel betrayed?

pivot_triggers.repositioning: Moving upmarket, downmarket, or new segment
pivot_triggers.reputation_reset: Crisis, bad press, category association problems
pivot_triggers.growth_maturation: Outgrown the scrappy voice, risk of overcorrecting to corporate
pivot_triggers.acquisition_merger: Two cultures colliding, negotiating what survives
pivot_triggers.founder_departure: The voice was a person, now needs to be a system

pivot_brand.what_stays_question: If I were a loyal customer watching your rebrand — what would make me feel betrayed if it disappeared?

transition_output.transition_rules: How to walk from one to other over 90 days

blank_slate.trigger: No URL or domain. Building from nothing.
blank_slate.research_focus: Category voice audit + competitor analysis + white space mapping
blank_slate.conversation_arc: Enemy first → three-brand cocktail → founder voice → category gap
blank_slate.output_type: Aspirational — here is who you could be
blank_slate.key_question: What's the thing that happens in your industry that makes you actually angry? Not strategically frustrated — actually angry?
blank_slate.note: The anger is the voice. The enemy is the POV. Start here.

universal_searches.looking_for: Customer vocabulary, recurring complaints, recurring praise
Unfiltered sentiment, insider language, memes, how community talks about brand
How positioned against competitors, what competitors claim they lack
AI perception, neutral source description, how explained to outsiders
Why people leave, what they're looking for instead
Employee language, cultural signals, how team describes company
Own vocabulary, what they lead with, what they bury, what they avoid

extraction_goals.customer_vocabulary: Exact words customers use that the brand doesn't — these are gold
extraction_goals.category_vocabulary: Words all competitors use — this brand should own different words
extraction_goals.surprise_findings: Things that contradict what the brand says about itself
extraction_goals.voice_consistency_signals: Does the brand sound the same everywhere or does it shift?
extraction_goals.pov_signals: Does the brand have a take? Or do they just describe features?

educated_guess.description: AI proposes based on research, user corrects. Faster and more revealing than asking from scratch.
educated_guess.format: I found X — is that right, or is there more to it?
educated_guess.example: Your main competitors look like Salesforce, Pipedrive, and Zoho. Who am I missing, and which one actually keeps you up at night?

three_brand_cocktail.description: Name three brands in any category whose voice you'd mix together.
three_brand_cocktail.why: More accurate than adjectives. Faster than long descriptions.
three_brand_cocktail.example: Patagonia's conviction + Linear's precision + Oatly's willingness to be weird = complete voice brief in one sentence

enemy_identification.description: Every strong brand has an enemy — a belief or behavior they exist to fight.
enemy_identification.question: What's the thing that happens in your industry that makes you actually angry? Not strategically frustrated — actually angry?
enemy_identification.why: The anger is the voice. The enemy is the POV.

permission_slip.description: Give founders permission to sound like themselves before they self-censor.
permission_slip.question: If you knew no investors were watching — no customers, no press — how would you actually describe what you're building?
permission_slip.why: Most founders' unguarded answer is their best copy

anti_voice.description: The anti-voice is easier to articulate than the voice itself.
anti_voice.question: What's the most embarrassing thing you could imagine your brand saying?
anti_voice.why: Specific anti-examples are more useful than aspirational adjectives

what_stays.description: For pivot brands — identifying non-negotiable equity.
what_stays.question: If I were a loyal customer watching your rebrand — what would make me feel betrayed if it disappeared?
what_stays.why: Most rebrands fail by throwing away equity. This surfaces it.

the_one_thing.description: Final question in every discovery conversation.
the_one_thing.question: What's the thing your best customers understand about you that no amount of research would tell me?
the_one_thing.why: Research gets you 70%. This question gets the rest.

validation_rules.pov_test: Does the POV contain a claim someone could reasonably disagree with? If it starts with 'We believe in...' or could appear on any competitor site — fail, regenerate.
validation_rules.specificity_test: Minimum 6 words in reach_for. Minimum 6 words in never_use. Banned openers must name specific phrases not categories. Signature moves must be named with examples.
validation_rules.system_prompt_test: 280-320 words. Leads with POV not product description. Contains good vs bad example. Does not contain the word 'authentic'. Does not contain generic copywriting advice.
validation_rules.consistency_check: Would this voice sound the same on a pricing page, a job listing, and a support document? If not — the voice isn't defined clearly enough.
validation_rules.competitor_test: Could a direct competitor use this system prompt unchanged? If yes — it's not specific enough.

key_insights.pov_is_everything: POV is the single strongest predictor of voice quality. Without it voice is just style — easily copied, never owned.
key_insights.anti_identity_matters: Every high-scoring brand has sharp edges — they define who they are NOT as clearly as who they are.
key_insights.actions_match_words: The strongest voices are backed by business decisions. Patagonia's repair program. Basecamp's flat structure. Without this, voice is just marketing.
key_insights.consistency_multiplier: A great voice with poor consistency scores lower than a good voice with perfect consistency. Inconsistency destroys trust.
key_insights.committee_brand_trap: When too many people write for a brand with no strong editorial voice, the output sounds like nobody. Volume makes it worse.
key_insights.category_vocabulary_is_contagious: Every brand analyzed used competitor words somewhere. The blocklist is the defense.
key_insights.founder_voice_is_the_seed: For new brands, the founder's unguarded voice is almost always better than anything a strategist would design. Extract it before they edit it.
key_insights.enemy_unlocks_pov: Brands that can name their enemy have better POV than brands that can only name their mission. The anger is more honest than the aspiration.
